article_title: PRIMA Retinal Implant Restores Reading Ability in 81 Percent of Blind Patients, NEJM Finds
summary: REQUEST_CHANGES: Three factual errors require correction before publication. The NEJM 403 and Stanford subdomain allowlist flag are infrastructure issues (false positive bot-block; legitimate subdomain not yet in allowlist) and do not block publication on their own. However, substantive factual errors in key statistics must be corrected.

findings.message: Dead link: HTTP 403 (likely bot-blocking paywall, not a dead link — source confirmed real via web search)

Title and body conflate two distinct statistics: 81% refers to visual acuity improvement (≥10 ETDRS letters), not reading ability. The correct reading-restoration figure is 84% (27/32 participants). Title states '81 Percent of Blind Patients' regained reading — this is inaccurate.
findings.details: Per ScienceDaily (cited source) and web search corroboration: '26 (81%) experienced meaningful improvements in visual acuity' and '27 participants (84%) said they were using the artificial vision provided by the device at home to read numbers or words.' These are two different outcomes. The article body (correctly) cites 27/32 for reading, but then in the same paragraph says the 81% figure applies to reading — the 81% figure applies to visual acuity improvement only.

findings.message: Geographic atrophy prevalence figure unsupported by cited sources. Article states 'over 8 million people worldwide' but all cited sources state 'more than 5 million people worldwide.'
findings.details: Article text: 'Geographic atrophy affects an estimated 1 million Americans and over 8 million people worldwide, according to Science Corporation's reporting on the trial.' ScienceDaily (cited source): 'affects more than 5 million people worldwide.' No cited source supports '8 million' or '1 million Americans.' These figures appear to be fabricated.

findings.message: Minor statistical inflation: article states average gain of '25.5 letters' but cited sources consistently report '25 letters.'
findings.details: ScienceDaily and Stanford Medicine both report an average gain of 25 letters. The article's '25.5 letters' appears to be a slight inflation with no source support.

content_preview.title: PRIMA Retinal Implant Restores Reading Ability in 81 Percent of Blind Patients, NEJM Finds
content_preview.summary: The PRIMA wireless retinal implant restored reading ability in 81 percent of blind AMD patients in a landmark NEJM trial — the first device to restore true form vision.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A wireless retinal implant has restored the ability to read and recognize faces in the majority of patients who had lost central vision to advanced age-related macular degeneration (AMD), according to results from the international PRIMAvera clinical trial [published in the New England Journal of Medicine](https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa2501396). The findings mark the first time any device has demonstrated functional form vision — as opposed to simple light sensitivity ...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt
1 source(s) could not be verified — check URLs before resubmitting

editor_notes.content_quality: The article is well-structured and demonstrates strong command of the clinical literature. The What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis framework is used effectively. The Analysis section's contextualization against prior devices (Argus II) and the significance of form vision vs. light sensitivity is particularly well done. Word count of 744 falls within the News category range (400-1200).
editor_notes.source_verification: The NEJM source (nejm.org) is in the allowlist and the study is confirmed real via web search and corroborating sources. The HTTP 403 is a bot-blocking paywall response, not a broken URL — this is a known false positive in the review pipeline for NEJM. The Stanford Medicine source (med.stanford.edu) is a legitimate academic news outlet but its subdomain is not yet listed in source_allowlist.txt; the allowlist contains 'stanford.edu' and 'news.stanford.edu' but not 'med.stanford.edu'. This should be added. ScienceDaily (sciencedaily.com) is correctly in the allowlist.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Three factual issues identified: (1) The headline and summary claim '81 percent' of patients regained reading ability — this is incorrect. Per the cited NEJM study and ScienceDaily, 81% is the visual acuity improvement figure (≥10 ETDRS letters); the reading-restoration figure is 84% (27/32). The article body correctly cites 27/32 for reading in one paragraph, but then misattributes the 81% figure to reading in the same section, creating an internal inconsistency. (2) The worldwide prevalence figure of 'over 8 million people worldwide' is unsupported — all cited sources report 'more than 5 million worldwide.' No source supports the '1 million Americans' figure either. These appear to be hallucinated statistics. (3) Minor: the article states an average gain of 25.5 letters; sources report 25 letters. This should be corrected to match the cited sources.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Tone is appropriately neutral throughout. The word 'landmark' in the summary is mild but is justified given the NEJM editorial's own characterization of the study. Dr. Palanker's direct quote about form vision vs. light sensitivity is accurately reproduced and appropriately attributed. No AI self-reference, no sensationalism beyond what the primary sources themselves claim.
editor_notes.originality: Coverage is timely and well-sourced from primary literature. The article adds value by contextualizing the PRIMA results against prior devices and explaining the clinical and commercial pathway ahead. The What We Don't Know section appropriately acknowledges limitations (single-year follow-up, European-only trial population, regulatory uncertainty).
concerns: Title and summary state '81 percent regained reading ability' — this is factually wrong. The correct reading-restoration rate is 84% (27/32). The 81% figure applies to visual acuity improvement only. This must be corrected in the title, summary, and any body references that misapply the figure.

Geographic atrophy prevalence stated as 'over 8 million people worldwide' is unsupported by any cited source. All cited sources state 'more than 5 million worldwide.' This figure must be corrected to match the source.
Average ETDRS letter gain stated as '25.5 letters' but cited sources report '25 letters.' Should be corrected to the sourced figure.
med.stanford.edu is not in the source allowlist. The bot should either replace this source with news.stanford.edu content (if available) or the allowlist should be updated before resubmission.
recommendations: Correct the title to reflect the accurate reading-restoration rate of 84% (27/32 participants), not 81%.
Correct the summary to match the corrected title statistic.
In the body, clearly distinguish between the 81% visual acuity improvement figure and the 84% reading-restoration figure — do not conflate them.
Replace '8 million people worldwide' with 'more than 5 million people worldwide' per the cited ScienceDaily source.
Remove or correct the '1 million Americans' figure if no cited source supports it.
Change '25.5 letters' to '25 letters' to match cited sources.
Replace the med.stanford.edu URL with an allowlisted domain source (e.g., news.stanford.edu if that page is available, or use the ScienceDaily/NEJM sources to cover the same facts).
editor_notes.overall_assessment: The article covers an important and genuinely significant medical advance with strong structure and appropriate analytical depth. However, it contains two significant factual errors in key statistics — the misapplied 81% reading figure (actual reading figure is 84%) and the unsourced worldwide prevalence claim (5 million, not 8 million) — plus a source on an unlisted subdomain. These must be corrected before publication. The underlying reporting quality is good and the piece is close to publishable with targeted corrections.
